conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by asking: Do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to understand a bit more about your situation.

Are you responsible for Alex, meaning do you live with Alex and are you the person who looks after them (providing food, clothing, and generally caring for them)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me and I look after them - I provide all their food, clothing, and take care of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Now I need to ask about your residency status.

Do you normally live in the UK, and are you either a UK citizen or have the right to live in the UK (for example, through settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another immigration status that allows you to claim benefits)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Before I can make a final determination, I need to ask a few more questions about Alex's situation.

Is Alex under 16 years old, or if they are 16 or over, are they in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 8 years old, so yes, they're under 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to ensure I have the complete picture.

Is Alex currently living with you in your home, or are they in any special circumstances such as being in hospital, in care, or living with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in hospital for a few months now, but they still live with me normally - I mean, they're in there for treatment but I still regularly spend money on them, buying things they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, thank you for sharing that. Children being in hospital is an important circumstance I need to consider.

How long has Alex been in hospital - is it more or less than 12 weeks? And is the hospital stay paid for by the NHS, or are you paying for it privately?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a bit more than 12 weeks - about 15 weeks now. And yes, it's NHS, not private.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. I need to check the specific rules about children in hospital to give you accurate guidance. Let me read the relevant government guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided and the official guidance, I have one final important question:

Do you regularly spend money on Alex while they're in hospital - for example, on things like clothes, toiletries, toys, or other items they need?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I still buy things for Alex - clothes, magazines, their favorite snacks and treats, that sort of thing. I regularly spend money on what they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me and the official government guidance, I can now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news - you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's the summary of your situation:

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16) - meets the age requirement
✓ You are responsible for Alex and normally live with them - meets the responsibility requirement  
✓ You are a UK citizen living in the UK - meets the residency requirement
✓ **Important exception applies:** Although Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (which would normally affect Child Benefit), you continue to regularly spend money on Alex's needs (clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). According to government rules, this means you can continue to receive Child Benefit even after the 12-week hospital period.

You should inform HMRC about Alex's hospital stay if you haven't already done so, and make sure they're aware that you continue to regularly spend money on Alex. This will ensure your Child Benefit continues without interruption.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so meets the age requirement. You are responsible for Alex as their parent, living with them normally and providing for their care. You are a UK citizen living in the UK, which meets the residency requirements. Although Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 15 weeks) on the NHS, there is an exception to the usual rule that Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital. According to government guidance, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital beyond 12 weeks if you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex, buying clothes, magazines, snacks, and other items they need. Therefore, this exception applies to your situation, and you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK citizen living in the UK who is responsible for their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has been in NHS hospital care for more than 12 weeks, the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex's needs. Under the government rules, an exception allows Child Benefit to continue beyond the 12-week hospital limit when the parent regularly spends money on the child. The claimant meets all eligibility criteria including this exception.
